skill.name: tdd-workflow
skill.description: Test-Driven Development workflow principles. RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle.
skill.summary: Write tests first, code second using the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle for reliable software development.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The arrow character (→) was misidentified as shell backtick execution. References to 'crypto' and 'reconnaissance' are pattern matching errors on plain text documentation. This is a safe TDD methodology guide. External commands are legitimately used for running test suites.

content.value_statement: Developers waste time debugging code without tests. This skill enforces the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle to build reliable software with confidence.
actual_capabilities: Guide through RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle for each feature
Write failing tests before implementing code
Apply AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert) for test structure
Prioritize tests by importance (happy path, errors, edge cases)
Refactor code safely while keeping all tests passing
Coordinate multi-agent TDD workflow with specialized roles
limitations: Requires existing test framework setup in the project
Not suitable for exploratory prototyping or UI layout work
Cannot run tests directly without shell access
Effectiveness depends on quality of test assertions written

use_cases.title: New Feature Development
use_cases.description: Build reliable features by writing tests first, then implementing minimal code to pass each test.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building APIs or business logic

use_cases.title: Bug Fix with Regression Protection
use_cases.description: Write a test that reproduces the bug, fix it, then refactor with confidence that the bug stays fixed.
use_cases.target_user: Maintainers fixing issues in existing codebases

use_cases.title: Team Code Quality Improvement
use_cases.description: Use multi-agent TDD pattern where different agents write tests, implement code, and refactor.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads managing team development workflows

prompt_templates.title: Start TDD for New Function
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to implement a function that [describe behavior]. Write a failing test first following the RED phase of TDD. The test should describe the expected behavior clearly.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Starting a new feature with TDD

prompt_templates.title: Complete RED-GREEN-REFACTOR Cycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Let's implement [feature] using TDD. Step 1: Write a failing test. Step 2: Write minimal code to pass. Step 3: Refactor for clarity. Guide me through each phase.

prompt_templates.title: Apply AAA Pattern to Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this test and restructure it using the AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert). Identify what data needs setup, what code executes, and what outcome to verify.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Agent TDD Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a multi-agent TDD session for [feature]. Agent A writes the failing test, Agent B implements to pass, Agent C refactors. Coordinate the handoffs between phases.

output_examples.input: Implement a function to validate email addresses
output: RED Phase: Created test 'should accept valid email format' - test fails (no implementation yet)
GREEN Phase: Implemented minimal regex validation - test passes
REFACTOR Phase: Extracted validation logic to separate function, improved error messages

output_examples.input: Fix null pointer exception in user lookup
output: RED Phase: Test reproduces the null pointer exception - confirmed failure
GREEN Phase: Added null check before accessing user properties - test passes
REFACTOR Phase: Created reusable null-safe lookup helper used throughout codebase

best_practices: Always watch the test fail before writing implementation code - this validates the test works
Write one assertion per test to isolate failures and clarify intent
Commit after each complete RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle to maintain small, reviewable changes
anti_patterns: Writing code before the test fails - defeats the purpose of TDD and often means you don't understand requirements
Testing implementation details like private method names - tests should verify behavior, not internal structure
Multiple assertions per test - makes failures ambiguous and violates single responsibility principle

faq.question: What if my test doesn't fail on the first try?
faq.answer: This means either the test is not properly testing the behavior, or the code already exists. Review your test logic or check if the feature was already implemented.

faq.question: Can I skip TDD for simple changes?
faq.answer: Simple is subjective. If you're confident enough to skip the test, the change should be trivial to test anyway. TDD catches edge cases you might miss.

faq.question: How do I handle database or API calls in TDD?
faq.answer: Use mocks or fakes to isolate the unit under test. Test the logic, not the external dependency. Integration tests can verify real connections separately.

faq.question: What if TDD slows down my development?
faq.answer: TDD feels slower initially but reduces debugging and regression time. The investment pays off in code quality and confidence during refactoring.

faq.question: Should I use TDD for UI components?
faq.answer: TDD has lower value for pure UI layout. Use it for component logic and state management. Visual regression tools complement TDD for UI testing.

faq.question: How do I prioritize which tests to write first?
faq.answer: Start with the happy path (expected behavior), then error cases, then edge cases. Performance tests come last after functionality is complete.
